text.title: Docker and CI Integration
text.summary: Docker multi-stage build pattern for Poetry projects and GitHub Actions CI workflow with caching.

section.title: Docker Multi-Stage Pattern

rule.statement: In Docker builds, use a builder stage to run poetry export, then a final stage with pip install -r requirements.txt. Never run poetry install in the final production stage — it brings the Poetry runtime into the image.
rule.rationale: Poetry adds ~100MB to the image and is not needed at runtime. The export + pip pattern produces minimal images.

example.code:
FROM python:3.11-slim AS builder WORKDIR /app RUN pip install poetry==1.8.5 COPY pyproject.toml poetry.lock ./ RUN poetry export -f requirements.txt --output requirements.txt --without-hashes
FROM python:3.11-slim WORKDIR /app COPY --from=builder /app/requirements.txt . RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt COPY src/ ./src/ CMD ["python", "-m", "my_project.main"]
example.note: Pin the poetry version in the builder stage. poetry shell is removed in Poetry 2.0 — never call it in Dockerfiles.

section.reference: Full multi-stage Dockerfile for Poetry projects

rule.statement: Use snok/install-poetry@v1 in CI and cache the .venv directory keyed by OS + Python version + poetry.lock hash. This reduces install time from minutes to seconds on cache hit.
rule.rationale: Poetry's dependency resolution is the slowest CI step. Caching the resolved environment avoids re-running the solver on every push.

rule.statement: Use the POETRY_PYPI_TOKEN_PYPI environment variable in CI, not poetry config pypi-token.pypi $TOKEN. The env var is process-scoped; the config command writes to ~/.config/pypoetry/auth.toml which persists across runs in shared sandboxes.
rule.rationale: Token written to auth.toml in a shared CI environment is accessible to other jobs. Process-scoped env var leaks nothing beyond the current step.
